feed.title: Sarthak Tiwari
feed.subtitle: A software developer striving for perfect productivity.

entry.title: On mentoring
entry.summary: Been mentoring a few engineers more formally over the last six months. Some things that have actually worked: Positive reinforcement is underused. When someone does something well - catches an edge case in review, asks a clarifying question that changes the design, writes a runbook that future-them will thank - say so specifically. Not “great job”, but “the way you traced that back to the SLA ...

entry.title: Reading in 2026
entry.summary: Keeping the list open this year. No target number. I’ve noticed that when I’m counting, I optimize for short books. Which is fine, some of the best books are short. But it creates a bias I don’t want. Currently reading Midnight’s Children, which I’ve started and abandoned twice before. Third time seems to be working.

entry.title: The Ministry for the Future Review

link.title: The Ministry for the Future Review

entry.summary: One line review - The Ministry for the Future Kim Stanley Robinson writes about climate change the way nobody else does - with actual policy, economics, and the grinding institutional work of trying to change large systems. It’s not a thriller, not a disaster narrative, it’s something more like a systems analysis with characters attached. Dense, occasionally frustrating, and the most honest fi...

entry.title: What building with agents actually looks like

link.title: What building with agents actually looks like

entry.summary: Eight months since my last post on this. Some things learned from actually building with agents: The reliability problem is more tractable than I expected if you scope tasks tightly. Agents fail on vague instructions for the same reason junior engineers fail on vague instructions. Specificity helps a lot. The observability story is still weak. When an agent makes ten tool calls to complete a ...

entry.title: The God of Small Things Review

link.title: The God of Small Things Review

entry.summary: One line review - The God of Small Things Arundhati Roy’s prose is almost too much at times and then suddenly exactly right. The structure - circling the same moment from different distances - should be exhausting but it’s not. The Kerala it describes, the caste and class mechanics operating quietly underneath every interaction, feels true in the way that only fiction about real things can. On...
